items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: In 1H NMR, what does the n+1 rule predict?
items.correct: A proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons will appear as a peak split into n+1 sub-peaks.
items.misconception: Splitting tells you how many protons are in the same chemical environment as the one giving the peak.
items.random_wrong: The number of peaks equals the total number of protons in the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse splitting (about NEIGHBOURING protons) with the number of equivalent protons producing the peak (which affects peak area/integration, not splitting).

items.q: When a stress shifts an equilibrium, does the system fully undo the change?
items.correct: No — Le Chatelier's principle says the system PARTIALLY opposes the change; equilibrium shifts to a new position that minimises but doesn't eliminate the disturbance.
items.misconception: The system shifts to completely cancel the change and return concentrations to their original values.
items.random_wrong: The system shifts to amplify the change because reactions always proceed in the disturbed direction.
items.why: Students treat Le Chatelier as a perfect homeostat, missing that the new equilibrium reflects the new conditions and only partially counters the disturbance.

items.q: Sodium has atomic number 11. Its electron configuration is:
items.why: Correct answer: 2,8,1. “2,9” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An atom of Mg loses 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺. Its number of protons is:
items.why: It's 12. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many neutrons in an atom with mass number 23 and atomic number 11?
items.why: The real figure is 12 neutrons. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
